t: In DSWPD morning; in Advanced evening.
– physiological low dose (0.3–5 mg); in DSWPD evening (time critical); in jet lag.
Chronotherapy — gradual shift of sleep timing.
Shift work — strategic resting, dark room, caffeine timing.
Non-24-hour in blind patients — tasimelteon (Hetlioz, FDA approved).
10,000 lux × 30–60 minutes; timing adjusted for circadian discrepancy.
With intervention, most patients improve; chronic form requires ongoing strategy.
Myth 1: "The adolescent is 'lazy' and can get up early"
DSWPD is the neurobiological basis of circadian delay during puberty.
melatonin regulates circadian phase, hypnotic effect is minimal; timing selection is critical.
Myth 3: "There is no treatment for shift work insomnia"
Strategic rest, light therapy, melatonin, caffeine timing improves.
Myth 4: "Jet lag resolves on its own"
1 day approximately for 1 hour of shift; acceleration with light + melatonin.
WHO. ICD-11. 7A40 Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders. 2024.
Auger R.R. et al. AASM Clinical Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Intrinsic Circadian Rhythm Sleep-Wake Disorders. J Clin Sleep Med 2015;11(10):1199–1236.
